bofs.name: KeyloggerRawInput
bofs.description:
Keylogger based on RegisterRawInputDevices

INFO:
First run: the BOF registers for raw input using RegisterRawInputDevices to start capturing keystroke into Windows internal raw-input buffer (max. 10.000 WM_INPUT messages).
Next run: the BOF drains and processes all pending WM_INPUT messages from the raw-input buffer and print the results to the beacon console.

ARGUMENTS:
[<option>]: specify one of the following options: "run" (start the keylogger or collect keystroke results), or "stop" (stop the keylogger).

USAGE:
keyloggerrawinput <option>

EXAMPLES:
keyloggerrawinput run
keyloggerrawinput stop

args.name: cmd
args.description: Specify `run` to start the logger or collect results. Specify `stop` to stop recording.

bofs.name: ForceLockScreen
bofs.description:
Force the lock screen of the current user session.

INFO:
Force the lock screen of the current user session.

USAGE:
forcelockscreen

bofs.name: AddLocalCert
bofs.description:
Add a self signed certificate to a specified local computer certificate store.

INFO:
Add a (self signed) certificate to a specified local computer certificate store. For example, add a certificate to the "Trusted Root Certification Authorities" (ROOT) folder on the local computer. All the properties are filled in based on the metadata in the certificate except the "Friendly Name" property. This property needs to be set manually as an argument.

ARGUMENTS:
[<path to certificate file>]: the path on your own attacker system to the certificate.cer file.
[<store name>]: the certificate store name (like ROOT) to import the certificate into.
[<friendly name>]: the name that is set in the "Friendly Name" property.

USAGE:
addlocalcert <path to certificate.cer file> <store name> "<friendly name>" 

EXAMPLES:
addlocalcert C:\Users\operator\Documents\examplecert.cer ROOT "Microsoft Root Certificate Authority 2010"

args.name: certFileBytes
args.description: A certificate to add to the store (import expects `X509_ASN_ENCODING` format).

args.name: store
args.description: Certificate store to import to.

args.name: friendlyName
args.description: Specify a friendly name to be shown in the "Friendly Name" property.

bofs.name: EnumSysmon
bofs.description:
Verify if Sysmon is running.

INFO:
Verify if Sysmon is running. This can be done by checking the registry or by enumerating Minifilter drivers and search for one that is associated with Sysmon.

OPTIONS:
[reg]: search the registry to check if Sysmon is present on the system and return the Sysmon service PID if active.
[driver]: list all the Minifilter drivers on the system to check manually (requires elevated privileges).

USAGE:
enumsysmon <reg | driver>

args.name: action
args.description: Method to check for sysmon: `reg` or `driver`.

bofs.name: EnumRWX
bofs.description:
Enumerate RWX memory regions in a target process.

INFO:
Find processes that already have memory allocated for read/write/execute (like most .NET processes)

OPTIONS:
[pid]: target process to enumerate

USAGE:
enumrwx <pid>

args.name: pID
args.description: PID of process to scan.

bofs.name: DelTaskScheduler
bofs.description:
Delete a scheduled task (local and remote support).

INFO:
Delete a scheduled task on the current system or a remote host.

ARGUMENTS:
[taskName]: The name of the scheduled task.
[hostName]: The FQDN of the remote host or leave empty for the current system.

USAGE:
deltaskscheduler <taskName> <(optional) hostName>

EXAMPLES:

deltaskscheduler TestTask DB01.example.local

args.name: taskName
args.description: Name of the tast.

args.name: hostName
args.description: The FQDN of the host (default: empty string for the current system).

bofs.name: DllEnvHijacking
bofs.description:
BOF implementation of DLL environment hijacking.

INFO:
This tool will setup a hidden file structure, move an already on disk present malicious proxy DLL to the new system32 folder, hide the proxy DLL, modify the SYSTEMROOT environment variable, run the vulnerable binary as a spoofed process to execute the malicious DLL, and reset the original SYSTEMROOT environment variable so the beacon keeps working as intended.

OPTIONS:
[<new sysroot dir>]: the new directory name as a path that will be used as the new SYSTEMROOT variable like C:\Data\ (make sure the directory path ends with \).
[<malicious DLL name>]: the name of the malicious DLL that will be loaded by the vulnerable binary (e.g. mswsock.dll).
[<path to mal. DLL folder>]: the path on the target system to the folder were the malicious DLL is stored (don't add the DLL name and end the path with a \).
[<name of vulnerable binary>]: the name of the vulnerable binary that will be executed and loads the malicious DLL (e.g. hostname.exe).
[<pid parent proc>]: the process ID of the parent process under which the vulnerable binary will run as a child.

USAGE:
dllenvhijacking <new sysroot dir> <malicious DLL name> <path to mal. DLL folder> <name of vulnerable binary> <pid parent proc>

args.name: sysrootPath
args.description: Path that will be used as the new SYSTEMROOT variable (e.g. `C:\Data\`).

args.name: proxyDll
args.description: Name of the malicious DLL that will be loaded.

args.name: inputDllSrcPath
args.description: Path on target system that contains the malicous DLL (from `proxyDll`, do not append the DLL name).

args.name: targetProc
args.description: Name of the vulnerable binary to execute.

args.name: pid
args.description: Parent proces ID under which the process will be spawned.

bofs.name: EnumFiles
bofs.description:
Search for matching files based on a word, extention or keyword.

INFO:
Search for matching files based on a word, extention or keyword in the file content. Wildcards are supported . Keyword matching only works for text based files.

ARGUMENTS:
[<path to directory>]: specify a path to the directory from which to start searching (recursive searching supported).
[<search pattern>]: specify a single word or extention to search for (support wildcards).
[<keyword>]: leave empty OR specify a keyword to search for in text based files (support wildcards).

USAGE:
enumfiles <path to directory> <search pattern> <(optional) keyword> 

EXAMPLES:
enumfiles C:\Users\RTO\Documents *.xlsx
enumfiles C:\Users\RTO *login*.* username
enumfiles C:\Users\RTO *.txt *pass*

args.name: lpDirectory
args.description: Directory from which to start searching.

args.name: lpSearchPattern
args.description: Single word or extention to search for (supports wildcards).

args.name: keyword
args.description: Leave empty, or specify a keyword to search for in text based files (supports wildcards).

bofs.name: EnumDrives
bofs.description:
Enumerate drive letters and its type.

INFO:
Enumerate drive letters and its type.

USAGE:
enumdrives

bofs.name: AddExclusion
bofs.description:
Add a new exclusion to Windows Defender for a folder, file, process or extension.

INFO:
Add a new exclusion to Windows Defender for a folder, file, process or extension.

ARGUMENTS:
[<exclusion type>]: specify one of the following exclusion types: path (file/folder), process, extension.
[<exclusion data>]: specify the data to add as an exclusion.

USAGE:
addexclusion <exclusion type> <exclusion data>

EXAMPLES:
addexclusion path C:\Users\Public\Downloads
addexclusion process C:\Windows\System32\example.exe
addexclusion extension *.xll

args.name: exclType
args.description: Exclusion type: `path`, `process` or `extension`.

args.name: exclData
args.description: String that describes the exclusion condition.

bofs.name: CredPrompt
bofs.description:
Start custom Windows credential prompt.

INFO:
Start Windows credential prompt in an attempt to capture user credentials. Entered credentials are returned as output. The prompt is persistent so the victim can't cancel/close the prompt or enter an empty password. Any user attempt to do so is shown in the output. Finally, a timer for the prompt is set to make sure the beacon will return at some point.

ARGUMENTS:
[<title>]: a custom window title.
[<message>]: a custom message set in the window.
[<timer>]: number in seconds after how long the prompt should auto close. Default is set to 60.

USAGE:
credprompt <title> <message> <(optional) timer>

EXAMPLES:
credprompt "Microsoft Outlook" "Connecting to user@example.com" 60

args.name: title
args.description: Title of the prompt window.

args.name: message
args.description: Message to display in the prompt window.

args.name: timer_seconds
args.description: Number of seconds before auto closing the window.

bofs.name: EnumExclusions
bofs.description:
Check the AV for excluded files, folders, extentions and processes.

INFO:
Check the AV for excluded files, folders, extentions and processes. Currently only Windows Defender exclusions are supported.

USAGE:
enumexclusions

bofs.name: EnumShares
bofs.description:
List remote shares and there access level using a predefined list with hostnames.

INFO:

ARGUMENTS:
[<path to file>]: the path on your own attacker system to the file containing the list with predefined hostnames. Each hostname must be newline separated.

USAGE:
enumshares <path to hostname file>

EXAMPLES:

args.name: hostFileBytes
args.description: File containing one hostname or ip address per line.

[option]: specify one of the following options to request related security information from WSC: av (antivirus), fw (firewall), as (antispyware).

USAGE:

EXAMPLES:

args.name: option
args.description: Type of security products to query.

bofs.name: EnumLocalCert
bofs.description:
List all the stored local computer certificates from a specific store.

List all the stored local computer certificates from a specific store. Common store names are: ROOT, MY, TRUST, CA, USERDS, AuthRoot, Disallowed.

args.name: store

bofs.name: CaptureNetNTLM
bofs.description:
Capture the NetNTLMv2 hash of the current user.

INFO:
Capture the NetNTLMv2 hash of the current user. This is done by simulating a NTLM authentication exchange between a client and server to capture the NetNTLMv2 hash.

USAGE:
capturenetntlm

bofs.name: PasswordSpray
bofs.description:
Validate single password against multiple accounts using kerberos authentication.

INFO:
Validate single password against multiple accounts using kerberos authentication.

ARGUMENTS:
[path to username file]: the path on your own attacker system to a file containing the list with usernames. Each username must be newline separated.
[password]: the password to validate.
[domain]: FQDN of the domain.
[sleeptimer]: (optional) sleep timer in seconds to wait between each authentication attempt (default is 0).
[jitter]: (optional) jitter in percentage (default 0).

USAGE:
passwordspray <path to username file> <password> <domain> [opt <sleeptimer>] [opt <jitter>]

EXAMPLES:
passwordspray C:\Users\redmed\Documents\usernames.txt Welcome01 example.local 10 40
passwordspray C:\Users\redmed\Documents\usernames.txt Welcome01 example.local

args.name: usernameFileBytes
args.description: File containing usernames seperated by newlines (`\r\n`).

args.name: wPassword
args.description: Password to spray against the supplied users.

args.name: wDomain
args.description: FQDN of the domain to target.

args.name: sleepDuration
args.description: Time to wait between authentication attempts (default: `0`).

args.name: jitterPercent
args.description: Jitter to apply to the wait intervals (default: `0`).

Check current user activity based on the user's last input

USAGE:

bofs.name: BlindEventlog
bofs.description:
Blind Eventlog by suspending its threads.

INFO:
Blind Eventlog by suspending its threads. This technique requires elevated privileges.
Be aware that all events, from the period the threads were suspended, will be pushed to Eventlog the moment the threads are resumed.

OPTIONS:
[suspend]: find and suspend all Eventlog threads and disrupt its functionality
[resume]: find and resume all Eventlog threads and restore its functionality

USAGE:
blindeventlog <suspend | resume>

args.name: action
args.description: Action to take: `suspend` or `resume`.

bofs.name: SilenceSysmon
bofs.description:
Silence Sysmon by patching its capability to write ETW events to the log.

INFO:
Silence the Sysmon service by patching its capability to write ETW events to the log.
Restarting the Sysmon service or the system itself will clear the patch and Sysmon will resume working normally.
Altough this will not leave any traces in the log, there will be a time gap between the last and first new event.

OPTIONS:
[pid]: the process ID of the Sysmon service running on the system.

USAGE:
silencesysmon <sysmon pid>

args.name: pid
args.description: Process id of the sysmon process.

bofs.name: EnumTaskScheduler
bofs.description:
Enumerate and list all the scheduled tasks in the root folder.

USAGE:

EXAMPLES:

args.name: hostName
args.description: Hostname of the system to enumerate (default: empty string for the current system).

Find process and thread handle types between processes.

OPTIONS:

[h2p]: list all processes that have a handle to a specific process
[p2h]: list handles from a specific process to all other processes

Delete a local computer certificate from a specific store.

bofs.description:
Find running WebClient services.

USAGE:

args.description:
File containing hostsnames (seperated with `
`).

bofs.name: AddTaskScheduler
bofs.description:
Create a scheduled task (local and remote system support).

INFO:
Create a scheduled task on the current system or a remote host.

BASIC ARGUMENTS:
[taskName]: The name of the scheduled task.
[hostName]: The FQDN of the remote host or "" for the current system.
[programPath]: Path to the program that you want to run like: C:\Windows\System32\cmd.exe.
[programArguments]: Arguments that you want to pass to the program like: "/c C:\Windows\System32\calc.exe" or "" to leave it empty.
[triggerType]: The trigger that signals the execution like: onetime, daily, logon, startup, lock, unlock. For more information, check the TRIGGER OPTIONS below.

TRIGGER OPTIONS:
[onetime]: Create task with trigger "On a schedule one time".
[daily]: Create task with trigger "On a schedule daily."
[logon]: Create task with trigger "At log on" (requires admin privs if set for another user or all users).
[startup]: Create task with trigger "At startup" (requires admin privs).
[lock]: Create task with trigger "On workstation lock" (requires admin privs if set for another user or all users).
[unlock]: Create task with trigger "On workstation unlock" (requires admin privs if set for another user or all users).

TRIGGER SPECIFIC ARGUMENTS:
[startTime]: Start time of the trigger in format: 2023-03-24T12:08:00.
[expireTime]: Expiration time of the trigger in format: 2023-03-24T12:08:00.
[daysInterval]: Interval in number of days. For example: 1 or 3.
[delay]: Random time delay after the start time in which the trigger is hit. Use format "PT2H" for hours and "PT15M" for minutes.
[userID]: Specify the user for which the trigger is set in format: "DOMAIN\username" for domain users, "username" for local system users and "" for all users (requires admin privs if set for another user or all users).
[repeatTask]: Set "Repeat task every x minutes/hours" option in format "PT2H" with a duration of "Indefinitely".

USAGE:
addtaskscheduler <taskName> <(optional) hostName> <programPath> "<(optional) programArguments>" onetime <startTime> <(optional) repeatTask>
addtaskscheduler <taskName> <(optional) hostName> <programPath> "<(optional) programArguments>" daily <startTime> <(optional) expireTime> <(optional) daysInterval> <(optional) delay>
addtaskscheduler <taskName> <(optional) hostName> <programPath> "<(optional) programArguments>" logon <(optional) userID>
addtaskscheduler <taskName> <(optional) hostName> <programPath> "<(optional) programArguments>" startup <(optional) delay>
addtaskscheduler <taskName> <(optional) hostName> <programPath> "<(optional) programArguments>" lock <(optional) userID> <(optional) delay>
addtaskscheduler <taskName> <(optional) hostName> <programPath> "<(optional) programArguments>" unlock <(optional) userID> <(optional) delay>

EXAMPLES:
addtaskscheduler TestTask "" C:\Windows\System32\cmd.exe "/c C:\Windows\System32\calc.exe" daily 2023-03-24T12:08:00 2023-03-28T12:14:00 1 PT2H
addtaskscheduler NewTask DB01.example.local C:\Users\Public\Downloads\legit.exe "" logon Testdomain\Administrator
addtaskscheduler OneDrive "" C:\Data\OneDrive.exe "" unlock "" PT5M

args.name: taskName
args.description: Name of the scheduled task.

args.name: hostName
args.description: FQDN of the system to add the task to (default: empty string for current system).

args.name: programPath
args.description: Path to executable to start.

args.name: programArguments
args.description: Arguments to start the `programPath` with (default: empty string for none).

args.name: triggerType
args.description: Trigger for the scheduled task.

args.name: optionalArg1
args.description: See description for details.

args.name: optionalArg2
args.description: See description for details.

args.name: optionalArg3
args.description: See description for details.

args.name: optionalArg4
args.description: See description for details.

bofs.name: PSremote
bofs.description:
List all running processes on a remote host.

INFO:
Get a list of all processes running on the remote host.

USAGE:
psremote <FQDN or IP remote host>

args.name: hostName
args.description: FQDN or ip addres of the remote system

bofs.name: InjectPoolParty
bofs.description:
Inject listener shellcode in specified process and execute it via Windows Thread Pools.

INFO:
Inject listener shellcode in specified process and execute it via Windows Thread Pools.
The following execution variants are supported: TP_TIMER (variant 8) | TP_DIRECT (variant 7 | TP_WORK (variant 2).
The following beacon shellcode configuration is injected: x64, process, indirect. This can be changed in the .cna script.

ARGUMENTS:
[<variant>]: Windows Thread Pool execution variant: TP_TIMER | TP_DIRECT | TP_WORK (susceptible to slow execution time).
[<pid>]: Process ID of the target process.
[<listener>]: Beacon listener name.

USAGE:
injectpoolparty <variant> <pid> <listener>

EXAMPLES:
injectpoolparty TP_TIMER 1234 Shorthaul-HTTPS

args.name: option
args.description: Thread pool variant: `TP_TIMER`, `TP_DIRECT` or `TP_WORK`.

args.name: pid
args.description: The ID of the process to target.

args.name: code
args.description: Shellcode to inject into the remote process.

bofs.name: DllComHijacking
bofs.description:
Leverage DLL Hijacking by instantiating a COM object on a target host

INFO:
Instantiate a COM object on a target host that will start the associated process which is vulnerable to DLL Hijacking. This allows for (remote) code execution and is therefore a good lateral movement technique.
Reminder: place the proxy DLL in the correct location before running this tool.

ARGUMENTS:
[<CLSID>]: The CLSID of the COM class that is associated with the vulnerable process.
[<target>]: The FQDN, hostname or IP of the target host (can be remote- or the local host).

USAGE:
dllcomhijacking <CLSID> <target>

EXAMPLES:
dllcomhijacking {73FDDC80-AEA9-101A-98A7-00AA00374959} target.example.local

args.name: clsidString
args.description: CLSID to instantiate.

args.name: host
args.description: FQDN, hostname or IP address to trigger.

bofs.name: EnumLib
bofs.description:
Find loaded module(s) in remote process(es)

INFO:
Find a specific loaded module in all processes OR list all loaded modules in a specific process.

OPTIONS:
[search]: find all processes that have loaded a specific module (e.g. winhttp.dll or ws2_32.dll).
[list]: list all loaded modules in a remote process.

USAGE:
enumlib search <module name>
enumlib list <pid>

args.name: option
args.description: Action to execute: `search` or `list`.

args.name: pid
args.description: PID or Module name.

notes.msg: Command generator does not work for the `pid` argument as it can have type `z` or `i`.

bofs.name: HideFile

bofs.name: AddFirewallRule
bofs.description:
Add a new inbound/outbound firewall rule.

INFO:
Add a new inbound/outbound firewall rule using COM.

ARGUMENTS:
[<direction>]: specify "in" for inbound or "out" for outbound.
[<port>]: specify a single port (80) or port range (80-1000)
[<rule name>]: specify the name of the new rule.
[<rule group>]: specify the name of the rule group OR leave empty.
[<description>]: specify the description of the new rule OR leave empty.

USAGE:
addfirewallrule <direction> <port> "<rule name>" "<rule group>" "<description>"

EXAMPLES:
addfirewallrule in 80 "ExampleRuleName1" "ExampleGroup1" "Test rule"
addfirewallrule out 80-1000 "ExampleRuleName2"

args.name: directionOption
args.description: Direction of traffic: `in` or `out`.

args.name: w_localPorts
args.description: Port to apply the rule to

args.name: w_ruleName
args.description: Name of the new rule

args.name: w_ruleGroup
args.description: Name of the rule group to add the new rule to.

args.name: w_ruleDescription
args.description: Description to add to the new rule.
